creator: marklmi26-fbsd@yahoo.com
text:
RPi4B's can boot from a microsd card in the slot or from a usb device
(that might be a microsd card reader/writer via usb).

Was /dev/ufs/rootfs on a microsd card in the slot? Is that the only
configuration that fails with the error 19 in your context? Can
you try any of the other kinds of contexts and use it if it works?

There is not anyone (or any group of folks) with the appropriate
range and depth of background knowledge that actively maintains
RPi* support. Historically, such folks give up after a time. Using
a workaround may be the most likely path to putting the B0T RPI4B's
to use.

Another alternative is to explore trying to find a vintage of
RPi* firmware and/or u-boot that, say, 14.1-RELEASE FreeBSD will
tolerate on a B0T RPi4B. Finding such would then allow some
exploration of, say, the live variation on the .dtb content
differences to see if any of those contribute to what the FreeBSD
RELEASE does not handle. It is messier if the differences are
outside the live .dtb content. (Live-content is because the
firmware makes some system specific adjustments to the content that
comes from the .dtb files.)

(Historically I've been able to get u-boot to output a textual form
of the .dtb related device tree that it hands off to the UEFI loader.
Not that I remember the details for doing so from one time to the
next.)

I'll note that you can learn some about the vintage of the RPi*
firmware from the likes of (based on having booted the media
on some RPi* in order for the file paths shown to work):

# strings /boot/efi/start4.elf | grep VC_BUILD_ID_
VC_BUILD_ID_USER: dom
VC_BUILD_ID_TIME: 10:50:39
VC_BUILD_ID_VARIANT: start
VC_BUILD_ID_TIME: Mar 17 2023
VC_BUILD_ID_BRANCH: bcm2711_2
VC_BUILD_ID_HOSTNAME: buildbot
VC_BUILD_ID_PLATFORM: raspberrypi_linux
VC_BUILD_ID_VERSION: 82f3750a65fadae9a38077e3c2e217ad158c8d54 (clean)

And about the u-boot vintage from the likes of:

# strings /boot/efi/u-boot.bin | grep "U-Boot 202"
U-Boot 2023.07.02 (Nov 03 2023 - 06:00:41 +0000)

No claim that those would match what you would see. The .dtb files,
for example, do not have such identifying text that I know of.
